name: The Pearl Tax Tables
shortDescription: A careful set of harbor tax tables used for pearl, shell, bead, and luxury cargo valuations. The margins are crowded with conversion notes, fee exceptions, and small warnings about which inspectors count by weight and which count by reputation.
flavorText: “A pearl may be round, but its tax never is.”

name: guild license
shortDescription: A stamped license proving that its bearer may trade under a guild’s rules, protections, and watchful accounting. Buyers trust it, rivals resent it, and guards tend to read it twice when the seal looks too freshly pressed.
flavorText: “Permission is cheaper than a fine, provided you buy it before the fine.”

name: painted fan
shortDescription: A folding fan painted with bright market scenes, court flowers, or little ships on a calm sea. It is light, elegant, and useful for more than heat; a sharp merchant can hide impatience, amusement, or a signal behind it.
flavorText: “Some bargains are closed with a handshake. Better ones begin behind a fan.”

name: miniature harbor painting
shortDescription: A palm-sized harbor painting set in a narrow frame, showing masts, gulls, colored awnings, and a blue strip of open water. Travelers buy it as a souvenir, while homesick sailors buy it for the tiny promise that every voyage has a dock at the end.
flavorText: “Small enough for a pack, large enough to smell of salt.”

name: puppet mask
shortDescription: A carved and painted mask made for puppet stages, festival stalls, and traveling storytellers. Its exaggerated smile reads clearly from the back row, which makes it useful for comedy, satire, and the kind of criticism that survives by pretending to be a joke.
flavorText: “A wooden face can say what a living one should not.”

name: lute string set
shortDescription: A tidy coil of replacement lute strings kept in waxed paper to hold off damp air. Musicians value it before performances, taverns value it after rowdy nights, and merchants value it because a silent bard never draws a crowd.
flavorText: “The song ends when the string gives up.”

name: tin whistle
shortDescription: A simple tin whistle with a clear, bright note that cuts through market noise and road dust alike. Children learn on it, sailors carry it, and guards either hate it or secretly use one during long watches.
flavorText: “Cheap music is still music, unless played beside your stall all morning.”

name: painted card deck
shortDescription: A deck of painted cards with worn corners, lively suits, and a faint smell of lamp smoke. It suits gambling tables, tavern games, fortune tricks, and long caravan evenings where boredom is more dangerous than a small wager.
flavorText: “A deck remembers every hand, though it keeps its accusations quiet.”

name: fortune tiles
shortDescription: A pouch of marked fortune tiles used by street readers and superstitious travelers. The symbols are simple enough to learn quickly, but vague enough that a skilled reader can make any result sound expensive.
flavorText: “The future is clearest after payment.”

name: glowmoss bundle
shortDescription: A damp bundle of glowmoss tied in soft cord, giving off a faint greenish shine in shade. Miners, cellar keepers, and night fishermen prize it as gentle light that does not smoke, spark, or complain about the dark.
flavorText: “Not all lanterns need flame. Some only need patience and wet stone.”

name: frostroot
shortDescription: A pale root that stays cool even under warm cloth, prized by apothecaries and ice-cellar cooks. Cut thin, it numbs the tongue; stored whole, it helps preserve delicate goods during short, urgent journeys.
flavorText: “It grows where winter forgets to leave.”

name: dream poppy pod
shortDescription: A dried poppy pod speckled with silver dust and sold with more caution than fanfare. Herbalists treat it as a delicate ingredient, while fortune tellers and poets speak of it in whispers that make sensible buyers ask for a receipt.
flavorText: “Dreams are cheaper in pods, but harder to wake from.”

name: glasswing beetle husk
shortDescription: A translucent beetle husk with wings that catch light like thin colored glass. Jewelers, charm makers, and patient alchemists buy them for inlay work, though every handling risks turning treasure into glittering crumbs.
flavorText: “Beautiful things often arrive already empty.”

name: firefly dust vial
shortDescription: A tiny vial of powdered firefly glow, sealed tight to keep its shimmer from fading. It is used in inks, stage tricks, children’s lanterns, and emergency trail marks that only reveal themselves once the sun has given up.
flavorText: “A little night bottled for later.”

name: binding salt jar
shortDescription: A corked jar of coarse binding salt used in warding circles, oath rites, and stubborn preservation work. The grains clump when handled by liars, according to sellers who charge extra for the story.
flavorText: “Salt keeps meat from spoiling and promises from wandering.”

name: red vigor tonic
shortDescription: A bright red tonic with a peppery bite and a smell like bruised berries. Laborers take it before heavy work, duelists take it before boasting, and careful merchants water it down for customers with too much confidence already.
flavorText: “Courage in a bottle, stamina if you are lucky, hiccups if you are not.”

name: green mending cordial
shortDescription: A smooth green cordial sold for small hurts, sore joints, and travelers who insist they are perfectly fine while limping. It tastes of mint, bark, and cautious optimism.
flavorText: “Not a miracle, but enough to get you to one.”

name: seasick sailor remedy
shortDescription: A sharp-smelling remedy bundled for sailors, ferry passengers, and proud merchants who discover humility in rough water. The bottle is ugly, the taste is worse, and repeat buyers rarely complain.
flavorText: “The sea accepts tribute in many forms. This one is swallowed first.”

name: thornbite antivenom
shortDescription: A small antivenom flask labeled for thornbite stings and hedge-creature scratches. Field gatherers keep it close during berry season, when the best fruits grow exactly where the worst little things hide.
flavorText: “Every bramble has a price. Some demand blood before coin.”

name: whisper ink vial
shortDescription: A dark ink that dries nearly invisible until warmed by breath or candle heat. Couriers, lovers, spies, and overly dramatic accountants all claim legitimate reasons to buy it by the vial.
flavorText: “Words that hide usually have something profitable to say.”

name: glowchalk stick
shortDescription: A chalk stick that leaves faint shining marks on stone, wood, and old brick. It is prized by tunnel guides, night watch crews, and children who should not be trusted with it near freshly painted doors.
flavorText: “The line glows longer than the apology.”

name: moonthread spool
shortDescription: A spool of pale thread that glimmers softly under moonlight, used for robes, hidden stitching, and discreet ceremonial trim. Tailors like its beauty; smugglers like that it is nearly invisible by day.
flavorText: “Sew by night, sell by morning.”

name: pocket ward charm
shortDescription: A small ward charm meant to be carried in a purse, coat seam, or boot lining. Whether it turns away bad luck or merely reassures nervous travelers, it sells best before long roads and after bad omens.
flavorText: “Protection you can misplace is still protection.”

name: bottled breeze
shortDescription: A sealed bottle holding a little swirl of restless air that taps the glass when shaken. Sailors, performers, and stuffy noble households all find uses for it, though opening it indoors is a quick way to lose receipts.
flavorText: “Wind is free until someone learns to cork it.”

name: rain call seed
shortDescription: A blue-gray seed sold to farmers, shrine keepers, and desperate gardeners with dry wells. It may call rain when planted with the right words, though merchants wisely avoid promising which cloud will listen.
flavorText: “Hope grows fastest in drought.”

name: shrine candle
shortDescription: A small shrine candle poured from clean wax and scented with a modest trace of resin. Pilgrims buy them for prayers, merchants buy them for polite offerings, and innkeepers buy them because travelers forget matches.
flavorText: “A little flame can carry a very large request.”

name: votive ribbon
shortDescription: A narrow ribbon meant to be tied at shrines, bridges, sacred trees, or anywhere a promise needs a witness. Its value depends less on cloth than intention, which makes it light cargo with surprising emotional weight.
flavorText: “Knots remember what tongues later soften.”

name: pilgrim shell
shortDescription: A polished pilgrim shell worn on cords, packs, and walking staffs to mark a journey honestly taken. Some are bought before the road, some after, and a few by people who want the story without the blisters.
flavorText: “The shell says you traveled. Your boots say whether it agrees.”

name: blessing oil vial
shortDescription: A tiny vial of scented blessing oil used for doors, tools, lamps, and the occasional worried forehead. It carries temple respectability in a pocket-sized measure, provided the cork has not leaked into the buyer’s purse.
flavorText: “A drop for luck, two for fear, three for a very persuasive priest.”

name: temple incense cone
shortDescription: A pressed cone of temple incense with a clean, solemn fragrance that lingers in cloth and memory. Shrines burn it for rites, households burn it for peace, and merchants burn it when a stall has smelled too long of fish.
flavorText: “Holiness, conveniently stackable.”

name: oath cord
shortDescription: A braided cord used to bind written pledges, seal agreements, or mark sworn service. It is plain to the eye but powerful in custom, especially where courts trust knots nearly as much as signatures.
flavorText: “Paper records the promise. Cord dares it to wriggle free.”

name: dock knife
shortDescription: A short, practical dock knife with a salt-stained grip and a blade meant for rope, fish, canvas, and sudden trouble. It is not elegant, but no one unloading cargo in bad weather asks elegance to cut a knot.
flavorText: “A sailor’s answer to rope, rind, and argument.”

name: boar spear
shortDescription: A stout boar spear with a crossbar below the head, made for hunters who prefer danger at arm’s length. It also sells well to rural guards, farmhands, and anyone who has met a boar and learned respect quickly.
flavorText: “The crossbar is there because bravery keeps sliding forward.”

name: caravan sabre
shortDescription: A curved caravan sabre built for riders, guards, and merchants crossing long, exposed roads. Its scabbard is often dustier than its blade, which is a sign of either peace or very good negotiation.
flavorText: “A road sword should be seen often and used rarely.”

name: arrow bundle
shortDescription: A tied bundle of serviceable arrows with plain shafts and practical heads. Hunters, militia stores, and caravan guards buy them in groups, because nobody has ever returned from trouble wishing they had packed fewer arrows.
flavorText: “One arrow is a chance. A bundle is a plan.”

name: bodkin arrow case
shortDescription: A narrow case of bodkin arrows made for piercing rings, plates, and other expensive reasons to duck. The points are slim, hard, and carefully packed so they do not turn the carrier’s own bag into a lesson.
flavorText: “Armor changes the question. Bodkins answer politely.”

name: boiled leather vest
shortDescription: A stiff boiled leather vest shaped for guards, road wardens, and cautious traders. It is lighter than metal and easier to repair, though it creaks when new and tells everyone exactly when the wearer bends.
flavorText: “Protection that announces itself before you do.”

name: enamel parade shield
shortDescription: A parade shield faced with bright enamel and polished trim, too handsome for mud and too costly for careless use. It belongs in processions, halls, and ceremonies where being struck is less likely than being admired.
flavorText: “Some shields defend the body. This one defends the family name.”

name: market piglet
shortDescription: A lively market piglet bundled for sale with rope, straw, and an impressive talent for escaping attention at the worst moment. Farmers see future pork, children see a friend, and merchants see paperwork if it gets loose.
flavorText: “Small enough to carry, loud enough to regret.”

name: pack mule
shortDescription: A sturdy pack mule with patient eyes and a back made for bundles, crates, and bad roads. It is less glamorous than a horse, but many caravans owe their profit to animals that never cared about glamour.
flavorText: “A mule does not believe your schedule. It believes the hill.”

name: beehive box
shortDescription: A wooden beehive box with slatted sections and a waxy smell clinging to the seams. It is valuable to orchard keepers, candle makers, and anyone willing to accept that honey arrives with opinions.
flavorText: “Sweetness is never truly unguarded.”

name: songbird cage
shortDescription: A delicate cage for a songbird, built with thin bars, a small perch, and a handle meant for careful hands. Nobles buy it for music, travelers buy it for company, and the bird usually judges both.
flavorText: “A small prison, unless the song is payment enough.”

name: false bottom box
shortDescription: A plain box with a concealed lower compartment, useful for keepsakes, reserve coin, or documents that should not greet every curious hand. Its best feature is looking too boring to steal first.
flavorText: “Honest wood, dishonest geometry.”

name: unmarked pearl pouch
shortDescription: A soft, unmarked pouch sized for pearls, beads, or other small valuables that benefit from silence. It has no seal, no embroidery, and no helpful clue for thieves who prefer labeled work.
flavorText: “The richest pouch is often the one pretending not to be.”

name: loaded dice
shortDescription: A pair of loaded dice with just enough weight hidden inside to embarrass chance. They are sold quietly, denied loudly, and discovered only when someone wins too often in a room full of poor losers.
flavorText: “Luck is lighter on one side.”

name: forged toll pass
shortDescription: A forged toll pass copied from a real road seal with dangerous confidence. It may save coin at a sleepy gate or cost far more at an alert one, making it less a document than a wager folded flat.
flavorText: “The cheapest road is sometimes the one that ends at questioning.”

name: lockpick roll
shortDescription: A rolled strip of narrow picks, tension hooks, and small tools for opening stubborn locks. Locksmiths call it professional kit, thieves call it opportunity, and merchants call it expensive if guards are listening.
flavorText: “Every locked door is a question. This roll carries too many answers.”

name: coded ledger scrap
shortDescription: A torn ledger scrap covered in numbers, marks, and trader shorthand that refuses to explain itself plainly. In the right hands it could reveal debt, shipment routes, or hidden profit; in the wrong hands it is just nervous paper.
flavorText: “A secret account never tears cleanly.”

name: bottle with a tiny storm
shortDescription: A thick glass bottle containing a tiny turning storm, complete with flashes, mist, and a faint rattle of impossible rain. Collectors love it, sailors distrust it, and sensible vendors keep it padded in straw.
flavorText: “Weather is easier to sell when it cannot reach full size.”

name: singing shell
shortDescription: A pale shell that hums softly when held near the ear, shifting between sea-song, wind, and something almost like words. Pilgrims treasure it as a sign, children as a wonder, and merchants as a crowd-gatherer.
flavorText: “The sea keeps singing even after it has been carried inland.”
